claim: Electric motors produce mechanical torque through the interaction of electrical current and magnetic fields, governed by Fleming's left-hand rule and the formula F = BLI.
Magnets supply the invisible magnetic field lines necessary for the electromagnetic interaction that produces mechanical force in motors.
Reluctance motors produce torque through magnetic alignment and changing magnetic reluctance rather than permanent magnet fields, using ferromagnetic rotor material.
Switched reluctance motors and synchronous reluctance motors operate without permanent magnets, using a simple ferromagnetic rotor with no windings or conductors.

Switched reluctance motors without permanent magnets exhibit drawbacks including high torque ripple, torque pulsations, noise at low speed, and lower torque density compared to permanent magnet motors.
Magnet-free motors are desirable for EV and robotics applications due to rugged structure, adaptability to harsh environments, fault tolerance, and simple construction.

Virtual Magnet Synchronous Motor (VMSM) technology by Vimag Labs uses software and power electronics to generate and control the rotor's magnetic field in real time, eliminating the need for permanent magnets.
SESM (Separately Excited Synchronous Motor) technology uses wound rotors excited through inductive coupling (not slip rings) to eliminate permanent magnets while reducing losses by ~15%.
Magnet-free motor platforms generally sacrifice some efficiency compared to permanent magnet motors, which directly affects EV range.
~95% of all EVs use Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM) requiring NdFeB rare earth magnets.
Induction motors work by inducing rotor currents via alternating stator fields rather than using permanent magnets or direct excitation, and have dominated industrial applications for over a century.
Synchronous reluctance motors meet IE4 efficiency standards with high power density, low rotor inertia, and no rare earth materials.
Hybrid excitation switched reluctance motors (HESRMs) offer control flexibility, high torque/power density, and broad speed range operation for EV applications.
China controls >90% of rare earth refined supply (Nd, Pr, Dy, Tb), and NdPr prices surged +105% to ~$100/kg in 2026, creating supply chain vulnerability.
A typical single-motor BEV contains ~550g rare earth oxides in the traction motor alone, while a conventional ICE car contains only ~140g.
Alternative non-magnetic motion principles exist including electrostatic, electret, and piezoelectric actuators that operate without magnetic fields.
Vimag Labs VMSM technology is being piloted with 2-wheeler and passenger vehicle manufacturers and targets 200-600 kW industrial systems, robotics, defense, and cooling applications.
